evidence_text: SWE-EVO contains 48 software-evolution tasks drawn from 7 mature open-source Python projects.
source_excerpt: "Constructed from release notes of seven mature open-source Python projects, SWE-EVO comprises 48 tasks" and later "In total, SWE-EVO comprises 48 such evolution tasks across 7 open repositories."
note: The source presents this as the benchmark’s final composition, not as a projected target.
caveat: The source presents this as the benchmark’s final composition, not as a projected target.

evidence_text: The mean SWE-EVO gold patch edits 610.5 lines across 20.9 files and 51.0 functions.
source_excerpt: Table 1 reports: "Gold Patch # Lines edited 610.5", "# Files edited 20.9", and "# Func. edited 51.0".
note: These are micro-averages across task instances rather than per-repository averages.
caveat: These are micro-averages across task instances rather than per-repository averages.

evidence_text: SWE-EVO task test suites average 81.4 FAIL_TO_PASS tests and 874.0 total tests per instance.
source_excerpt: Table 1 lists under Tests: "# Fail to Pass 81.4" and "# Total 874.0".
note: The source frames these test counts as part of the benchmark’s verification burden.
caveat: The source frames these test counts as part of the benchmark’s verification burden.

evidence_text: SWE-EVO retains only candidates with at least one FAIL_TO_PASS test and discards candidates with installation or runtime errors.
source_excerpt: "We retain only instances that exhibit at least one FAIL_TO_PASS test ... ensuring a measurable behavioral change attributable to the required evolution. We additionally discard candidates that trigger installation or runtime errors under the benchmark environment."
note: This describes an execution-based filtering rule used during dataset construction.
caveat: This describes an execution-based filtering rule used during dataset construction.

evidence_text: In SWE-EVO, the model receives a release-note item plus the complete pre-release codebase and must output a patch implementing the change.
source_excerpt: "A model is provided with (i) a release-note item that specifies the intended change ... and (ii) a complete codebase at the pre-release commit. The model must produce edits to the codebase that implement the described change."
note: The source distinguishes the generated "model patch" from the ground-truth "gold patch."
caveat: The source distinguishes the generated "model patch" from the ground-truth "gold patch."

evidence_text: Resolved Rate counts an instance as solved only if all FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS tests pass under the model patch.
citation: Thai et al., SWE-EVO, Section 3.2 Resolved Rate
source_excerpt: "The Resolved Rate of an instance is equal 1 if all tests in both FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS are PASSED; otherwise, it is 0."
note: This is a strict binary success criterion.
caveat: This is a strict binary success criterion.

evidence_text: Fix Rate gives partial credit for fixed FAIL_TO_PASS tests, but assigns the entire instance a score of 0 if any PASS_TO_PASS test regresses.
citation: Thai et al., SWE-EVO, Section 3.2 Fix Rate
source_excerpt: "The Fix Rate measures progress by computing what fraction of FAIL_TO_PASS tests the model successfully fixes. Crucially, it also enforces a regression constraint: if the model’s patch breaks any PASS_TO_PASS test ... the entire instance receives a score of 0."
note: This metric preserves a no-regression condition while exposing partial progress that the binary Resolved Rate hides.
caveat: This metric preserves a no-regression condition while exposing partial progress that the binary Resolved Rate hides.

evidence_text: With release-note plus PR/issue context, gpt-5-08-07 resolved 18.75% of SWE-EVO tasks in OpenHands and 20.83% in SWE-agent, versus 65.00% on SWE-bench Verified.
source_excerpt: Table 2 reports for "gpt-5-08-07 (medium reasoning)": "18.75" resolved for OpenHands, "20.83" resolved for SWE-agent, and "65.00" on SWE-bench Verified.
note: The source uses this comparison to show a performance gap between SWE-EVO and SWE-bench Verified.
caveat: The source uses this comparison to show a performance gap between SWE-EVO and SWE-bench Verified.

evidence_text: In the release-note-only setting, gpt-5-08-07 resolved 14.58% of SWE-EVO tasks in OpenHands and 16.67% in SWE-agent.
source_excerpt: Table 3 lists for "gpt-5-08-07 (medium reasoning)": "% Resolved 14.58" under OpenHands and "% Resolved 16.67" under SWE-agent.
note: These results are lower than the same model’s scores in the release-note-plus-PR/issue setting.
caveat: These results are lower than the same model’s scores in the release-note-plus-PR/issue setting.

evidence_text: Adding PR/issue context improved SWE-EVO results only modestly, according to the authors.
source_excerpt: "Finally, providing PR/issue context yields measurable but modest gains, suggesting that even with detailed and fully specified context, agents still struggle to reconstruct the correct release-note-aligned implementation change."
note: This is an author interpretation of the release-note-only versus context-augmented comparison rather than a single tabulated metric.
caveat: This is an author interpretation of the release-note-only versus context-augmented comparison rather than a single tabulated metric.

evidence_text: For gpt-5, more than 60% of unresolved SWE-EVO trajectories were labeled as instruction-following failures.
source_excerpt: "Among the GPT-series, the strongest model (gpt-5) almost never fails due to syntax or tool errors; instead, its unresolved cases are dominated by Instruction Following mistakes (over 60%)."
note: This result comes from an LLM-as-a-judge failure-mode analysis of unresolved SWE-agent trajectories.
caveat: This result comes from an LLM-as-a-judge failure-mode analysis of unresolved SWE-agent trajectories.

evidence_text: Instances solved less often were associated with more pull requests, with mean PR counts of 14.84, 6.71, 3.57, and 1.67 across difficulty groups from hardest to easiest.
source_excerpt: "Figure 6(a) shows a clear monotonic trend ... The average PR counts corresponding are: 14.84, 6.71, 3.57, and 1.67 across the four groups."
note: The source presents PR count as a practical proxy for SWE-EVO instance difficulty.
caveat: The source presents PR count as a practical proxy for SWE-EVO instance difficulty.

evidence_text: The authors state that SWE-EVO currently covers only Python projects, uses release notes as specifications, and has 48 instances, which they say limits statistical power for fine-grained comparisons.
source_excerpt: "Constructed from release notes of seven mature open-source Python projects, SWE-EVO comprises 48 tasks" and later "In total, SWE-EVO comprises 48 such evolution tasks across 7 open repositories."
note: The source presents this as the benchmark’s final composition, not as a projected target.
caveat: The source presents this as the benchmark’s final composition, not as a projected target.
